title: Say It So They Get It
developmental_frame: At 11–12 a child can hold a listener in mind — they're starting to notice when someone doesn't follow them, though they don't yet reliably adjust. They know a lot about the things they love and will happily info-dump; the growth edge is shaping that flood so someone else can actually receive it. Short, performable tasks with a real listener beat abstract 'communication skills' talk.
authentic_value_meaning: Value here is a real person's face lighting up with 'oh, I get it now'. Making something you understand land in someone else's head is a genuine gift — and at this age it's mostly given to people they know: a parent, a friend, a younger sibling.

motivation_lever.mechanism: The child explains something they genuinely love — a game, a hobby, an animal — to a real person who honestly doesn't know it, and finds out whether that person actually understood. The live reaction (confusion or the click of understanding) is the motivator; you can't fake your way past a real listener's blank face, and landing it feels great.

artifact: A 2-minute explainer (performed live or recorded as a short video) that makes one real listener genuinely understand something they didn't before — plus that listener's honest 'did you get it?' verdict.
success_criteria: A real listener can restate the main idea in their own words afterward.
The explainer starts from something the listener already knew.
The child cut at least one thing that was interesting to them but confusing to the listener.

lessons.title: Start where they already are
lessons.concept: People understand new things by hooking them onto things they already know. Start from the listener's world, not yours — a bridge from something familiar to something new.
lessons.real_example: Explaining chess to someone new? 'It's like a battle where every soldier moves in its own special way' lands better than opening with 'the knight moves in an L'. The battle idea is already in their head.
lessons.apply_task: Pick your topic. Write one sentence that connects it to something your listener already knows and likes. That's your opening bridge.
lessons.reflection_prompt: What does your listener already know that you can build on? If you don't know, that's your first question to ask them.
lessons.feedback_task: Try your opening bridge on your real listener and watch: did they nod, or look lost? Adjust.
lessons.teach_back_task: Show a friend how to make a 'bridge' opening for a topic *they* care about.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: AI can suggest analogies if you're stuck — a fair nudge. But the best bridge uses what *this specific listener* knows, and only you know them. Don't hand your listener a stranger's analogy.
success_criteria: An opening line exists that links the topic to the listener's existing knowledge.

lessons.title: One idea at a time
lessons.concept: When you love a topic, everything feels important and it all rushes out at once — and the listener drowns. Great explaining is putting ideas in a line, one clear step after another.
lessons.real_example: A good recipe never says 'do everything now'. It's step 1, step 2, step 3. Explaining works the same way: land one idea, then the next.
lessons.apply_task: List everything you want to say, then number them into an order where each step makes sense only after the one before. Cross out any that don't earn a place.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Which idea *must* come first for the rest to make sense? Getting the order right is half the battle.
lessons.feedback_task: Read your ordered steps to someone. Ask where they got lost — that's usually where two ideas got crammed together.
lessons.teach_back_task: Help someone put their own jumbled explanation into a clear order.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: You can ask AI to help sort your points into an order, then check the order yourself against your listener. If a step only makes sense to you and not to them, the order is still wrong — trust the human, not the tidy list.
success_criteria: The explanation is broken into an ordered sequence with at least one idea cut.

lessons.title: Show it, don't just say it
lessons.concept: A picture, a demo, or a quick example lands harder than words alone. Showing turns a vague idea into something the listener can actually see.
lessons.real_example: Explaining how a lever works? Don't just describe it — grab a ruler and a pencil and lift a book. Now they *see* it, and they'll remember.
lessons.apply_task: Find one place in your explainer to show instead of tell — a drawing, an object, a quick demo. Build it into your 2 minutes.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Where in your explanation did the listener's eyes glaze? That's the spot that most needs a show-not-tell.
lessons.feedback_task: Do the explainer with your 'show' moment. Ask the listener which part stuck most — was it the shown bit?
lessons.teach_back_task: Challenge a friend to add a 'show' moment to their explanation and compare before/after.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: AI can suggest what to demonstrate, but the actual showing — the object, the drawing, your hands — is yours to make real. A demo you performed beats a clever one you only described.
success_criteria: The explainer includes at least one concrete demonstration or visual.

lessons.title: Deliver it and read the room
lessons.concept: The final skill is watching your listener while you talk and adjusting — slowing down at a frown, moving on at a nod. An explainer is a two-way thing, not a speech at someone.
lessons.real_example: A good teacher notices the class going quiet-confused and stops to re-explain. They're reading faces, not just reciting.
lessons.apply_task: Deliver your full 2-minute explainer to your real listener. Watch their face and adjust live at least once.
lessons.reflection_prompt: Where did you have to slow down or change course? Those spots show you what's genuinely hard to explain.
lessons.feedback_task: Afterward, ask your listener to say the main idea back in their own words. If they can, you succeeded. If not, find where it broke.
lessons.teach_back_task: Teach someone the trick of 'ask them to say it back' as the real test of any explanation.
lessons.ai_judgment_note: This is a *no-AI* delivery — it's a human moment between you and a real listener. Reading a real face and adjusting is the whole skill, and no tool can stand in that spot for you.
success_criteria: The explainer was delivered live and adjusted at least once mid-way.
The listener restated the main idea in their own words.
